questionText: Prove the identity $(a^2 + b^2)(c^2 + d^2) = (ac + bd)^2 + (ad - bc)^2$, and hence that the product of two sums of two squares is itself a sum of two squares.

steps.explanation: We prove the two sides are equal by expanding each and comparing; this is a direct algebraic proof.
Multiplying out every pair of terms gives four products.

steps.description: Expand the first square on the right.
steps.explanation: Squaring $ac+bd$ produces two squares and a cross term $2abcd$.

steps.description: Expand the second square on the right.
steps.explanation: Squaring $ad-bc$ produces two squares and the opposite cross term $-2abcd$.
We sum the two expansions to assemble the full right-hand side.
The mixed terms are equal and opposite, so they cancel exactly — this is why the identity works.
After cancellation only the four square terms remain.
The left-hand side and the simplified right-hand side contain exactly the same four terms.
Since both sides expand to the same expression, the identity is proved for all real $a,b,c,d$.

steps.description: Interpret the two factors as sums of squares.
steps.explanation: Each factor on the left is a sum of two squares by construction.

steps.description: Read the right-hand side as a sum of squares.
steps.explanation: The right-hand side is manifestly a sum of two squares, with integer bases when $a,b,c,d$ are integers.

steps.workingLatex: (\text{sum of 2 squares}) \times (\text{sum of 2 squares}) = \text{sum of 2 squares}
steps.explanation: The identity shows the product of any two sums of two squares can itself be written as a sum of two squares.

steps.workingLatex: \text{identity proved; product is a sum of two squares}
steps.explanation: Hence the algebraic identity holds and the product of two sums of two squares is a sum of two squares.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Expanding, both sides equal $a^2c^2+a^2d^2+b^2c^2+b^2d^2$ (the $\pm2abcd$ cancel), so the identity holds. Its right-hand side is a sum of two squares, proving the product of two sums of two squares is a sum of two squares. $\blacksquare$
